_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and burden per WHO Soil-Transmitted Helminthiases 2024 and Global Burden of Disease 2021. Pathophysiology and treatment per Hotez et al. Lancet 2008 (PMID 18804113) and Loukas et al. NEJM 2016 (PMID 26580418) on Necator americanus and Ancylostoma. Albendazole-mebendazole comparative efficacy per Keiser & Utzinger JAMA 2008 (PMID 18430913). Iron deficiency anemia in pregnancy per Brooker et al. Lancet 2008. WHO 2020 STH Roadmap. CDC DPDx and CDC Yellow Book.

h1Title: Hookworm Infection: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hookworm infection is a chronic intestinal parasitism caused by the blood-feeding nematodes Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale (and rarely Ancylostoma ceylanicum), acquired when infective larvae penetrate intact skin during contact with fecally-contaminated soil. The World Health Organization estimates 472-740 million people are infected globally, concentrated in low-income tropical and subtropical regions with poor sanitation. Each adult worm consumes 0.03-0.26 mL of host blood per day; moderate-to-heavy worm burdens drive iron-deficiency anemia, hypoalbuminemia, and impaired growth and cognition in children. Pregnancy carries particular risk, with hookworm contributing meaningfully to maternal anemia and low birth weight. A single dose of oral albendazole 400 mg or mebendazole 500 mg cures more than 70-90% of cases, and WHO-coordinated mass drug administration to school-age children has reduced morbidity substantially in dozens of endemic countries.

keyStats.prevalence: 472-740 million people infected globally (WHO 2024 estimate); incidence concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America
keyStats.demographics: Children aged 5-15 and women of reproductive age carry the highest morbidity burden; rural agricultural and mining populations at greatest risk
keyStats.avgAge: Infections accumulate from early childhood; peak intensity in late childhood through young adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 1.5-3 million disability-adjusted life-years (DALYs) lost annually (GBD 2021); hookworm is the leading parasitic cause of iron-deficiency anemia worldwide

definition: Hookworm infection (ICD-10: B76) is a soil-transmitted helminth infection caused by Necator americanus (the dominant species in the Americas, sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific) or Ancylostoma duodenale (in the Middle East, North Africa, India, and southern Europe historically), and occasionally Ancylostoma ceylanicum (Southeast Asia, an emerging zoonotic and human-adapted species). The life cycle begins when infective filariform (L3) larvae in warm, moist soil penetrate human skin — typically through the dorsum of the foot during barefoot walking. Larvae enter the venous circulation, traverse the right heart and pulmonary capillaries, ascend the bronchial tree, are swallowed, and mature in the small intestine where adult worms (8-13 mm) attach to villi using cutting plates (Necator) or teeth (Ancylostoma). Adult females release 5,000-30,000 eggs per day passed in feces; eggs hatch into rhabditiform larvae in soil and mature to filariform larvae within 5-10 days. Each adult worm consumes 0.03-0.26 mL of host blood daily through extracellular digestion of villi and arterial bleeding from feeding sites, producing iron and protein loss proportional to worm burden. Disease manifestations are dose-dependent: light infection causes minimal symptoms; moderate-to-heavy infection produces iron-deficiency anemia, hypoproteinemia, growth faltering, and cognitive deficits. Hookworm is one of three major soil-transmitted helminths (alongside Ascaris and Trichuris) targeted by the WHO neglected tropical disease elimination roadmap.

typesClassification.description: The dominant species globally, accounting for 80-90% of human hookworm. Adult lifespan 3-5 years (longest documented 15+ years). Cutting plates rather than teeth attach to villi. Endemic across sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific.

Historically dominant in the Middle East, North Africa, the Indian subcontinent, southern Europe, and parts of China. Two pairs of teeth attach to villi. Capable of oral and vertical transmission (transmammary in breastfeeding). Adult lifespan typically 1-2 years.

Zoonotic hookworm of dogs and cats with increasing recognition as a human-adapted parasite in Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Thailand, Malaysia). Causes typical hookworm disease and is detectable on stool PCR.

Caused by skin penetration of zoonotic Ancylostoma braziliense or Ancylostoma caninum larvae from dog or cat feces, common in Caribbean and Latin American beachgoers. Larvae cannot complete the human life cycle so they wander cutaneously, producing a serpiginous pruritic eruption; not associated with anemia.

Symptomatic primary infection with very heavy oral exposure to Ancylostoma duodenale; produces pharyngeal irritation, cough, dyspnea, eosinophilia, and high mortality if untreated, particularly described historically in Japanese mine workers.

primarySymptoms: Intense pruritic, erythematous papular eruption (ground itch) at the site of larval skin penetration, usually on the dorsum of the foot, within minutes to hours of soil contact.

Migratory wheezing, cough, and low-grade fever during pulmonary larval migration 1-2 weeks after exposure — Löffler-like syndrome with peripheral eosinophilia and transient infiltrates on chest imaging.
Vague upper abdominal pain, nausea, anorexia, and intermittent diarrhea 4-6 weeks after infection as adult worms establish in the small intestine.
Progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea, pallor, and reduced exercise tolerance from iron-deficiency anemia in moderate-to-heavy chronic infection.
Geophagia (pica for earth), pagophagia (ice craving), and koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) in long-standing iron deficiency.
Growth faltering, stunting, and impaired cognitive performance in chronically infected school-age children.
Edema (pedal and periorbital) from hypoalbuminemia in very heavy infections or chronically malnourished hosts.
Hematochezia or melena rare except in massive infection; chronic occult blood loss is typical.
earlyWarningSigns: Pruritic, papular rash on the feet a day or two after walking barefoot on soil in an endemic area (ground itch)
Unexplained eosinophilia (>500/µL, often >1500/µL) on routine blood count in a returning traveler or migrant from a tropical region
Anemia in a child or pregnant woman from a hookworm-endemic region, especially when iron-deficient and unresponsive to oral iron alone
Recurrent vague abdominal pain and intermittent diarrhea in a school-age child living in or visiting an endemic region
Migratory wheezing or cough with peripheral eosinophilia during the first months after returning from sub-Saharan Africa or South Asia
emergencySigns: Severe symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin under 6 g/dL) with tachycardia, dyspnea, lightheadedness, or chest pain — requires transfusion and inpatient evaluation
Heart failure from chronic severe anemia in pregnancy or childhood — urgent obstetric or pediatric admission
Massive gastrointestinal bleeding in very heavy infection — rare but reported in mining and agricultural workers
Acute respiratory distress with eosinophilic infiltrates and hypoxia (Löffler-like syndrome) during pulmonary larval migration
Wakana disease (acute hookworm syndrome) with pharyngeal edema, dyspnea, and hypotension after heavy oral Ancylostoma exposure

causes.cause: Skin penetration by filariform (L3) larvae from contaminated soil
causes.description: The dominant route of transmission for Necator americanus and most Ancylostoma duodenale infection. Walking barefoot on fecally-contaminated warm, moist soil in agricultural fields, around latrines, in mines, or on plantations allows larvae to penetrate intact skin (typically the dorsum of the foot). Larvae enter venous circulation, migrate via lungs, and mature in the small intestine 4-6 weeks after exposure.

Ancylostoma duodenale (uniquely among human hookworms) can also infect by ingestion of larvae on contaminated food or water, producing direct intestinal infection without pulmonary migration. Heavy oral infection produces Wakana disease.

Ancylostoma duodenale larvae can arrest in skeletal muscle during early pregnancy and reactivate at parturition, passing to nursing infants in breast milk. Documented in China and Africa; partly explains hookworm-associated infant anemia.

causes.cause: Lack of safe sanitation and shoe wearing
causes.description: Open defecation and lack of latrines deposit eggs in soil where they mature into infective larvae. Routine barefoot or sandal wearing in endemic rural environments allows skin contact. WHO estimates 700+ million people still practice open defecation; STH transmission is concentrated in these communities.

Subsistence farmers, agricultural laborers, miners, sand and gravel workers, tea plantation workers, and waste handlers face repeated cumulative exposure, sustaining high worm burdens into adulthood and driving sustained morbidity in working-age adults.

causes.cause: Warm, humid tropical and subtropical climate
causes.description: Filariform larvae survive optimally in 23-33 °C with adequate moisture. Sandy or loamy soils, shaded areas, monsoon rains, and irrigation create the ecological conditions needed to maintain transmission. Hookworm is virtually absent above 35° latitude in cold dry regions.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in a tropical or subtropical endemic region
riskFactors.description: Sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, the Pacific islands, parts of South and Central America, and the Caribbean carry the highest prevalence. Within endemic regions, prevalence is concentrated in rural agricultural communities.

riskFactors.factor: Open defecation and lack of safe sanitation
riskFactors.description: Open defecation deposits eggs in soil; lack of toilets and waste disposal sustains transmission. Sanitation coverage below 50% predicts community hookworm prevalence above 30% in cluster analyses.

riskFactors.factor: Habitual barefoot walking or open sandals on contaminated soil
riskFactors.description: Skin penetration of filariform larvae is the dominant transmission route. Shoe wearing reduces individual risk substantially in cluster-randomized trials.
Children spend more time barefoot and in soil, acquire worms cumulatively, and bear the greatest growth and cognitive consequences. Peak intensity is often in late childhood and adolescence.

Pregnant women with hookworm have higher rates of maternal anemia, low birth weight, and preterm birth. WHO recommends single-dose albendazole after the first trimester in endemic regions to reduce these risks.

riskFactors.factor: Subsistence farming, mining, or plantation work
riskFactors.description: Continual occupational contact with contaminated soil maintains high worm burdens. Tea, rubber, and rice plantation workers, miners, and brick-makers are repeatedly identified as high-risk.
Pre-existing iron and protein deficiency amplifies hookworm-related anemia; addressing both nutrition and parasitism is necessary for full recovery.

affectedDemographics: Global: 472-740 million people infected (WHO 2024 estimate); the largest single parasitic cause of iron-deficiency anemia.
Sub-Saharan Africa: prevalence 25-40% in many rural communities, dominated by Necator americanus.
South Asia (India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka): mixed Necator and Ancylostoma transmission; prevalence reduced substantially in school-age children under WHO-supported deworming programs.
Southeast Asia and the Pacific: persistent transmission in Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, Indonesia, and the Pacific islands; Ancylostoma ceylanicum increasingly recognized in Cambodia.
Latin America: focal transmission in northeast Brazil, Caribbean, Central American, and Amazonian communities.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines exposure history, peripheral blood count showing eosinophilia with iron-deficiency anemia, and microscopic identification of characteristic eggs on stool examination. Hookworm eggs (60-75 µm, thin-shelled, oval, with 4-8 cell morula or fully developed larva by the time stool reaches the lab) are indistinguishable between Necator and Ancylostoma on routine microscopy; PCR or larval culture is required for species identification. The WHO-recommended Kato-Katz thick smear quantifies egg burden (eggs per gram of feces) and stratifies severity: light <2,000 epg, moderate 2,000-3,999, heavy ≥4,000. Multiple stool samples on consecutive days substantially improve sensitivity in light infection. PCR-based assays (multiplex PCR for soil-transmitted helminths) have become the most sensitive method and now distinguish species, including Ancylostoma ceylanicum. Peripheral blood eosinophilia (often 500-3,000/µL) is common but non-specific. Iron studies (low serum ferritin, low transferrin saturation, microcytic hypochromic indices) and stool occult blood support the diagnosis of chronic blood loss. Differential diagnosis includes other soil-transmitted helminths (Ascaris, Trichuris, Strongyloides), schistosomiasis, malaria-related anemia, nutritional anemia, and inflammatory bowel disease. WHO-recommended diagnostic workflow in endemic areas focuses on stool microscopy and population-level screening; in returning travelers from tropical regions, screening also covers schistosomiasis and Strongyloides.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy with Kato-Katz thick smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and quantifies hookworm eggs; stratifies infection intensity for treatment and surveillance
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh or appropriately preserved stool sample; a 41.7 mg smear is examined under low power and eggs counted. Sensitivity rises with three samples on consecutive days. Result within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool concentration techniques (formol-ether sedimentation or Mini-FLOTAC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Improves sensitivity for light infections and is used in reference laboratories
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same stool sample processed with concentration steps; result within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highest sensitivity; distinguishes Necator from Ancylostoma and identifies Ancylostoma ceylanicum
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool sample submitted to a reference lab; results within 3-7 days. Increasingly used in travel medicine and high-prevalence research settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with peripheral smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies microcytic hypochromic anemia and peripheral eosinophilia (often the first clue in returning travelers)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results within hours. Microcytic indices (low MCV, MCH) support iron-deficiency pattern.

diagnosticTests.test: Iron studies (serum ferritin, transferrin saturation, total iron-binding capacity)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms iron-deficiency anemia consistent with chronic blood loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; ferritin can be falsely elevated by concurrent inflammation, so paired CRP measurement is useful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports chronic intestinal blood loss diagnosis in moderate-heavy infection
Hatches eggs to larvae for species identification and to detect Strongyloides co-infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool sample incubated for 5-7 days at reference lab; species identification by larval morphology.

treatmentOverview: First-line therapy for confirmed or suspected hookworm infection is a single oral dose of albendazole 400 mg or mebendazole 500 mg in patients aged 1 year and older; this regimen is endorsed by WHO for both individual treatment and mass drug administration. Albendazole single-dose cure rates of 72-80% and egg-reduction rates of 89-95% are reported (Keiser & Utzinger JAMA 2008 meta-analysis), with mebendazole performing slightly less well (cure 15-67%, egg reduction 80-90%) — leading WHO to favor albendazole as first-line. Triple-dose albendazole over three days improves cure rates to 90-95% in research settings but the single dose is preferred for population-scale programs. Pyrantel pamoate 11 mg/kg (max 1 g) for 3 days is an alternative where benzimidazoles are unavailable. Ivermectin alone has limited efficacy against hookworm but is used in combination with albendazole or mebendazole when co-infection with Strongyloides is suspected. Concurrent iron supplementation (typically ferrous sulfate 65-130 mg elemental iron daily) is essential when anemia is present; symptomatic patients with hemoglobin under 6 g/dL or hemodynamic compromise require transfusion. Treatment in pregnancy follows WHO guidance: albendazole or mebendazole single dose is recommended after the first trimester to reduce maternal anemia and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Mass drug administration to school-age children (and where prevalence above 50%, also to preschool children and women of reproductive age) is the WHO-endorsed public-health strategy and has reduced national prevalence in dozens of countries. Persistent egg-positivity after treatment requires repeat dosing and consideration of drug resistance, which is increasingly reported in veterinary parasitology and now being monitored in human STH programs.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 400 mg single oral dose (adult or child >1 year)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO first-line treatment. Inhibits microtubule synthesis in the parasite. Taken with a fatty meal to enhance absorption. Common adverse effects mild (transient abdominal discomfort, headache, nausea); rare hepatotoxicity. Avoid in first trimester pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 72-80%; egg-reduction rate 89-95% in WHO meta-analysis

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole 500 mg single oral dose (adult or child >1 year)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative benzimidazole. Lower systemic absorption than albendazole. Used in WHO mass drug administration programs.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 400 mg daily for 3 days
medicalTreatments.description: Extended dosing for confirmed heavy infection, suspected drug failure, or individual treatment in clinical settings. Same safety profile as single dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 90-95%; preferred in heavy infection and individual case management

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrantel pamoate 11 mg/kg (max 1 g) once daily for 3 days
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative where benzimidazoles are unavailable or contraindicated. Causes spastic paralysis of the worm. Safe in pregnancy beyond first trimester.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate 70-87%; useful where albendazole resistance suspected

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily or equivalent elemental iron 100-200 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory adjunct in any patient with anemia. Continued for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores. Vitamin C improves absorption; calcium and tea reduce it.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hemoglobin rise of 1-2 g/dL within 4-8 weeks when anthelmintic and iron given together; without anthelmintic, response blunted by ongoing blood loss

medicalTreatments.name: Blood transfusion (packed red cells)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin under 6 g/dL with hemodynamic compromise) or anemic heart failure in pregnancy. Bridge to anthelmintic and iron replacement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid restoration of oxygen delivery; not a definitive treatment

alternativeTreatments.name: Albendazole-ivermectin combination
alternativeTreatments.description: Used when Strongyloides co-infection cannot be excluded or in research settings showing improved efficacy against multiple soil-transmitted helminths; particularly relevant for trichuriasis co-infection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Community-based mass drug administration
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO-endorsed annual or biannual single-dose albendazole to school-age children (and, in high-prevalence settings, preschool children and women of reproductive age). Reduces community burden and morbidity even when individual cure is incomplete.

whySeeSpecialist: Most uncomplicated hookworm infection is managed by primary-care and pediatric services using single-dose albendazole and iron. Tropical medicine or infectious disease referral is appropriate for atypical presentations, suspected drug resistance, returning travelers with co-infections (schistosomiasis, Strongyloides, malaria), or severe complications including symptomatic anemia and pulmonary syndrome.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a primary-care or pediatric service comfortable with deworming protocols. For returning travelers, refer to a travel and tropical medicine clinic capable of multiplex stool PCR, serology for Strongyloides and schistosomiasis, and management of mixed infections.
hospitalCriteria: Access to stool microscopy with Kato-Katz technique
Reference parasitology or multiplex PCR availability
Tropical and travel medicine outpatient clinic
Pharmacy supply of albendazole and mebendazole
Hematology and transfusion services for severe anemia
keyFacilities: Primary-care and pediatric clinics in endemic regions
Travel medicine and tropical medicine outpatient services
Public-health units delivering mass drug administration
preventionStrategies: Wear closed shoes when walking on potentially contaminated soil; the single highest-impact personal measure.
Install and use safe latrines; community sanitation coverage above 75% substantially reduces hookworm transmission.
Wash hands with soap after toilet use and before food preparation.
Participate in school- or community-based deworming programs when offered, even in the absence of symptoms.
Avoid defecating or allowing children to defecate in open soil near homes, fields, or play areas.
Boil or chemically treat drinking water in areas where Ancylostoma duodenale transmission is documented.
lifestyleModifications: Take prescribed albendazole or mebendazole as a single oral dose with food.
Continue iron supplementation for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores.
Avoid tea, coffee, and dairy products within 1-2 hours of iron tablets (they impair absorption).
Take vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, guava) with iron-containing meals to improve absorption.
Use shoes consistently after treatment to prevent re-infection.
Return for follow-up stool examination 2-4 weeks after treatment to confirm clearance.
Disclose travel and soil-exposure history to any clinician evaluating eosinophilia, anemia, or vague gastrointestinal symptoms.

recommended: Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, liver, lentils, beans, dark leafy greens, fortified cereals)
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, guava, strawberries) eaten with iron sources to enhance absorption
Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) during recovery from hookworm-associated hypoproteinemia
Vitamin A- and folate-rich foods (orange and yellow produce, leafy greens) to support erythropoiesis
avoid: Tea, coffee, and high-calcium foods within 1-2 hours of iron supplementation
Unwashed raw produce in endemic regions
Untreated water from open sources in known Ancylostoma duodenale transmission areas
Self-medication with non-evidence-based herbal anthelmintics in place of proven therapy

exerciseGuidelines: Activity tolerance is limited by anemia rather than by direct parasite effects. As hemoglobin normalizes over weeks, gradually resume normal physical activity. Children with severe anemia at diagnosis should avoid strenuous exercise until hemoglobin recovery is documented.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed anthelmintic and iron supplementation on time.
Track symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, abdominal pain) and report worsening to the clinical team.
Maintain dietary iron and protein intake during recovery.
Use shoes consistently to prevent re-infection.
Keep stool samples for follow-up parasitology as instructed.
Attend follow-up hemoglobin and parasitology checks at 4 and 12 weeks.
prognosis: Prognosis is excellent with appropriate treatment. A single dose of albendazole 400 mg cures 72-80% of infections and reduces egg burden by 89-95%; persistent egg-positivity responds to repeat dosing or extended (3-day) regimens. Iron-deficiency anemia recovers fully when both parasite and iron deficiency are treated, with hemoglobin rising 1-2 g/dL within 4-8 weeks and full iron-store replenishment over 3-6 months. Growth catch-up in chronically infected children occurs within 6-24 months of effective treatment. The dominant long-term challenge in endemic settings is re-infection: without sanitation improvement and shoe-wearing, prevalence and worm burdens return toward pre-treatment levels within 6-18 months. WHO-supported mass drug administration of school-age children sustains lower community burdens but does not eliminate transmission. Drug resistance, while well-established in veterinary parasitology, remains uncommon but is monitored as an emerging concern in human STH programs. Travelers and migrants from endemic regions, with no further exposure, are usually cured by a single course of albendazole and remain disease-free.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms (abdominal pain, fatigue) improve within 1-4 weeks of treatment. Eosinophilia resolves within 3-6 months. Hemoglobin rises 1-2 g/dL within 4-8 weeks when iron is co-administered. Iron stores replenish over 3-6 months. Growth and cognitive recovery in chronically infected children occurs over 6-24 months.
complications: Iron-deficiency anemia with chronic fatigue and exercise intolerance — track hemoglobin and ferritin until both normalize; continue iron 3-6 months after hemoglobin recovery.
Hypoalbuminemia and edema in heavy infection — improves after deworming, iron, and protein nutrition.
Childhood growth faltering and impaired cognitive development — preventable and partially reversible with deworming and nutritional support.
Anemic heart failure or symptomatic anemia in pregnancy — requires urgent treatment with transfusion when hemoglobin under 6 g/dL with hemodynamic compromise.
Pulmonary larva migrans (Löffler-like syndrome) during primary infection — usually self-limited but occasionally severe.
Wakana disease (acute hookworm syndrome) after heavy oral Ancylostoma exposure — uncommon but historically fatal in mining communities.

supportResources.name: WHO Soil-Transmitted Helminthiases
supportResources.description: Authoritative epidemiology, treatment guidelines, and mass drug administration data for hookworm and other STHs.

supportResources.name: CDC DPDx — Hookworm
supportResources.description: Practical diagnostic and clinical guidance from CDC's parasitic disease laboratory.

supportResources.name: Children Without Worms
supportResources.description: Public-private partnership delivering deworming medication to school-age children in low-resource settings.

supportResources.name: CDC Yellow Book — Soil-Transmitted Helminths
supportResources.description: Travel-medicine guidance on hookworm and related parasites for clinicians evaluating returning travelers.

confusedWithConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Strongyloides stercoralis also causes eosinophilia and skin lesions (larva currens) but produces serpiginous, fast-moving cutaneous tracks rather than fixed ground itch, and can autoinfect to cause life-threatening hyperinfection in immunosuppressed hosts. Stool agar-plate culture or serology identifies it. Ivermectin (not albendazole alone) is first-line.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ascariasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ascaris lumbricoides eggs are larger and bile-stained on stool microscopy; adult worms can be visible in stool and on imaging. Heavy infection causes intestinal obstruction or biliary disease, not iron-deficiency anemia. Albendazole single dose treats both.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trichuriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trichuris trichiura produces lemon-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs and causes colitis, dysentery, and rectal prolapse in heavy infection rather than the iron-deficiency anemia of hookworm. Often co-endemic and treated alongside hookworm with albendazole.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosomiasis is acquired by skin contact with infected freshwater rather than soil; presents with hematuria (S. haematobium) or bloody diarrhea and hepatosplenomegaly (S. mansoni, S. japonicum), and is treated with praziquantel rather than benzimidazoles.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Iron-deficiency anemia from other causes (menstrual loss, diet, gastrointestinal bleeding, malabsorption) lacks travel or soil exposure, eosinophilia, and stool egg-positivity. Hookworm should be considered in any iron-deficient patient with relevant exposure who fails to respond to iron alone.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common soil-transmitted helminth globally; frequently coexists with hookworm and treated with the same albendazole regimen.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic soil-transmitted helminth treated alongside hookworm; less responsive to single-dose benzimidazole.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related soil-transmitted helminth with overlapping skin and pulmonary phases; requires ivermectin in addition to or instead of albendazole.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another neglected tropical disease causing eosinophilia in returning travelers; both targeted by integrated WHO control programs.

relatedConditions.name: Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Iron-deficiency anemia is the dominant clinical consequence of hookworm; both must be addressed for recovery.

relatedConditions.name: Malaria
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic in many regions; co-infection compounds anemia and morbidity, particularly in children and pregnancy.

faqs.question: What is hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Hookworm infection is a chronic parasitic disease caused by Necator americanus or Ancylostoma duodenale, intestinal worms that attach to the small intestine and feed on host blood. Infection is acquired when larvae in contaminated soil penetrate intact skin. WHO estimates 472-740 million people are infected globally.

faqs.question: How do people get hookworm?
faqs.answer: Most infections occur when larvae in fecally-contaminated warm, moist soil penetrate intact skin, usually on the dorsum of the foot during barefoot walking. Ancylostoma duodenale can also be acquired by ingesting larvae on contaminated food or water and from mother to infant through breast milk.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of hookworm?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include an itchy rash at the site of larval skin penetration (ground itch), migratory cough and wheezing during pulmonary larval migration, abdominal pain, intermittent diarrhea, and progressive fatigue and pallor from iron-deficiency anemia. Many light infections cause minimal symptoms.

faqs.question: How is hookworm diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by microscopic detection of hookworm eggs on stool examination using the Kato-Katz thick smear or concentration techniques. Multiplex PCR has higher sensitivity and distinguishes species. Eosinophilia and iron-deficiency anemia on blood tests support the diagnosis in patients with relevant exposure.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for hookworm?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is a single oral dose of albendazole 400 mg or mebendazole 500 mg in patients over 1 year old. Albendazole achieves cure rates of 72-80% and egg-reduction rates of 89-95%. Iron supplementation is added when anemia is present.

faqs.question: Can hookworm cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Each adult worm consumes 0.03-0.26 mL of host blood per day. Moderate-to-heavy worm burdens produce iron-deficiency anemia, hypoalbuminemia, and reduced exercise tolerance. Hookworm is the leading parasitic cause of iron-deficiency anemia worldwide, particularly in children and pregnant women.

faqs.question: How long does hookworm live in the body?
faqs.answer: Necator americanus adults typically live 3-5 years and have been documented to survive 15 years or longer in untreated hosts. Ancylostoma duodenale adults usually live 1-2 years. Egg shedding continues throughout the worm's lifetime without treatment, sustaining anemia and growth effects.

faqs.question: Can hookworm be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. Wearing closed shoes on potentially contaminated soil, using safe latrines, handwashing, and community sanitation improvements are highly effective. School- and community-based mass drug administration with single-dose albendazole reduces population burden and morbidity in endemic regions.

faqs.question: Is hookworm contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Hookworm is not directly contagious from person to person. Transmission requires fecally-contaminated soil where eggs hatch into infective larvae. Improving sanitation breaks the cycle. Sharing a household with an infected person does not transmit infection unless soil contamination also occurs.

faqs.question: Can children take albendazole for hookworm?
faqs.answer: Yes. Albendazole 400 mg or mebendazole 500 mg as a single oral dose is safe and effective in children 1 year and older. These medications are central to WHO-supported school-based deworming programs reaching hundreds of millions of children annually. Pediatric formulations as chewables are widely available.

faqs.question: Is hookworm treatment safe in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: WHO recommends single-dose albendazole or mebendazole after the first trimester to reduce maternal anemia, low birth weight, and preterm birth in pregnant women in endemic regions. The medication is avoided in the first trimester out of an abundance of caution. Iron and folate supplementation is concurrent.

faqs.question: What is ground itch?
faqs.answer: Ground itch is the intense pruritic, erythematous papular rash that develops on the feet within minutes to hours of larval skin penetration during contact with contaminated soil. It marks the entry point of filariform larvae and typically resolves over days as larvae enter the circulation.

faqs.question: Can hookworm affect children's growth and development?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic moderate-to-heavy hookworm infection in school-age children causes growth faltering, stunting, and impaired cognitive performance through chronic anemia, protein loss, and inflammation. Effective deworming and nutritional support produce measurable recovery over 6-24 months.

faqs.question: How is hookworm different from cutaneous larva migrans?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous larva migrans (creeping eruption) is caused by zoonotic Ancylostoma braziliense or Ancylostoma caninum larvae that cannot complete the human life cycle. They wander in the skin producing serpiginous tracks but never reach the intestine. It is treated with topical or oral albendazole or ivermectin; anemia does not occur.

faqs.question: Where is hookworm most common?
faqs.answer: Hookworm is concentrated in tropical and subtropical regions with poor sanitation: sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, the Pacific islands, and parts of South and Central America. WHO estimates 472-740 million infections globally, predominantly in rural agricultural communities.

faqs.question: Can I get hookworm from a beach?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous larva migrans from animal hookworm species (Ancylostoma braziliense, Ancylostoma caninum) occurs on beaches contaminated with dog or cat feces in the Caribbean, Central America, and parts of the southeast US. It produces an itchy serpiginous rash but does not cause anemia or intestinal disease.

faqs.question: Does hookworm come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Single-dose albendazole cures 72-80% of infections. Re-infection is common in endemic areas without sanitation improvement and shoe wearing; community prevalence can return to baseline within 6-18 months. Travelers and migrants without further exposure usually remain cured.

faqs.question: Do I need iron supplements with hookworm treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, when anemia is present. Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily or equivalent) is continued for 3-6 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replenish stores. Iron alone, without anthelmintic, produces blunted hemoglobin response because of ongoing blood loss.

faqs.question: Can hookworm be detected with a blood test?
faqs.answer: Blood tests can show eosinophilia and microcytic iron-deficiency anemia consistent with hookworm but are not diagnostic. Definitive diagnosis requires identification of eggs on stool microscopy or detection of parasite DNA by PCR. Specific serology is not routinely available.

faqs.question: How quickly do symptoms appear after exposure?
faqs.answer: Ground itch appears within minutes to hours of larval penetration. Pulmonary symptoms develop 1-2 weeks later as larvae migrate through the lungs. Adult worms reach the intestine after 4-6 weeks; clinical features of chronic disease (anemia, abdominal pain) typically take months to develop with cumulative worm burden.

faqs.question: Is there a hookworm vaccine?
faqs.answer: No hookworm vaccine is licensed yet. A recombinant vaccine targeting Na-GST-1 and Na-APR-1 antigens of Necator americanus has progressed through phase 1 and 2 trials but has not yet achieved population-protective efficacy. Until a vaccine is available, the cornerstone remains mass drug administration with albendazole and sanitation improvements.

symptomKeywords: itchy rash on feet after barefoot
